quote: Well, I mean, we do have Dominion Energy, for her yes. We do, yeah, we do have that. And we just paid paid, like, the the, energy bill just for the first month.
stewart_read: Paul volunteers that he just paid his first bill — this is the earliest available bill-flip opportunity in the call. Bucky's response is to ask for the dollar amount, which is correct data collection, but the emotional flip ('you just paid your first Dominion bill — that's the one that's going to keep going up every year') is never attempted. The bill is captured as data; it is not converted into alignment-against-the-utility.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky may have been holding the flip for after bill photo confirmation. Rejected — no flip occurs at any point in the call, including after the photo is received.
coaching_implication: Show Bucky the bill_anchoring section. The capture-and-drop pattern is present here in its clearest form: Paul hands Bucky the emotional lever ('we just paid our first bill') and Bucky asks for the number. The manager should ask Bucky: 'What would you say right after Paul tells you he just paid his first Dominion bill?' and work the flip from there.

quote: Hey, Paul. This is Bucky with I Am Solar. I got a request you're looking at solar for your home. How are you doing today?
stewart_read: Bucky includes a company name and a how-are-you-doing pause, which satisfies the structural minimum. However, the intro omits the Tesla/certified-installer legitimacy framing that the codex specifies for Tesla-sourced leads. On a warm, cooperative customer like Paul this may not have cost the call, but the trust anchor that answers 'is this a scam?' before the customer can ask it was never placed.
alternative_hypothesis: This may not be a Tesla-sourced lead — lead type is unknown. If it is a standard online form-fill lead, the standard intro applies and the Tesla framing is not required. The gap is the absence of any legitimacy anchor, not specifically the Tesla line.
coaching_implication: Ask Bucky what intro variant he uses and why. If this is a Tesla lead, walk through the codex framing. If it is a standard lead, the coaching point is narrower: the intro should still establish legitimacy quickly, even without the Tesla anchor. On a tougher customer than Paul, the missing trust anchor would have cost the call.

quote: Well, we just need a quote. We just need a quote for now.
stewart_read: Paul's 'we just need a quote' maps directly to the send_proposal_before_meeting rebuttal trigger phrases. Bucky's response redirects to the specialist model, which is structurally correct, but the execution is hedged ('they just leave you to your decision, I guess') and no flip is attempted. The rebuttal holds the line but does not create urgency or anchor value.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky may have judged that Paul was close enough to booking that a harder push would create friction. Plausible given Paul's cooperative tone, but the hedging language at 04:44 suggests the softness was not deliberate positioning — it reads as a confidence gap in the rebuttal.
coaching_implication: Pull the send_proposal_before_meeting rebuttal from the codex and walk through it with Bucky. The 'hard rule' note is explicit: stick to your guns. The 'I guess' hedge at 04:44 is the specific line to address — it undercuts the specialist's authority at the moment Paul needs to feel the appointment is worth his time.

quote: They so we actually have specialists, and they they actually either call you or bring it to you. And then you guys go over it together, and then they just leave you to your decision, I guess.
stewart_read: Within the send_proposal_before_meeting rebuttal execution, 'they just leave you to your decision, I guess' is a confidence gap. The codex framing emphasizes that proposals require full explanation and that consultants will explain everything so it makes sense — the value of the appointment is the specialist's expertise. Bucky's phrasing inverts this: the specialist shows up, presents, and then leaves Paul alone with a decision. That framing reduces the appointment's perceived value and reinforces Paul's low-urgency posture.
coaching_implication: This is a one-line fix. Have Bucky replace 'they just leave you to your decision, I guess' with something that anchors the specialist's role as an expert guide, not a passive presenter. The codex language ('our consultants will explain everything so it makes sense') is the target. Role-play the rebuttal until the hedge is gone.

stewart_read: Paul says 'Bye' and begins to disengage at 03:33 — a near-dropout caused by the ambiguity of Bucky's manager-check pause. Bucky had said 'I'm gonna talk to my manager about one thing' and 'I'll come back when I'm done talking to him,' which Paul reasonably interpreted as the call ending. The manager check itself is a scope-discipline signal: Bucky encountered a new-construction / single-month-bill edge case and escalated rather than bridging. The near-dropout is the downstream consequence.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky may have needed genuine manager clearance for a new-construction lead. Plausible, but the call-control risk of leaving Paul uncertain about whether the call was over was avoidable with a cleaner hold framing ('Stay with me — I'll be back in 30 seconds').
coaching_implication: Two coaching points in one moment. First: if a manager check is genuinely required, anchor the customer explicitly ('I need 30 seconds — stay on the line with me'). Second: ask Bucky what the manager check was for. If it was a new-construction qualification question Bucky should know the answer to, that is a product-knowledge gap to address directly.

quote: Yes. Yes. I want my wife and I both do.
stewart_read: Paul volunteers that his wife also has a credit score over 670, which signals she is financially involved in the household. Bucky does not follow up to ask whether she is on the title or whether she should be included in the appointment. The title question at 01:07 was answered with 'Just my bad. Yes. It is. Yes.' — Paul confirmed the title is in his name only. However, the wife's financial involvement is a co-decision-maker signal that Bucky does not surface or route.
alternative_hypothesis: If the title is confirmed in Paul's name only, the spouse-on-title protocol does not technically trigger. However, Paul's wife is clearly involved (she is the one forwarding the bill photo at 02:27, and Paul references her throughout). A co-decision-maker who is not on the appointment is a no-show risk regardless of title status.
coaching_implication: Walk Bucky through the spouse_decision protocol. The title question was asked correctly; the follow-through was not. When Paul says 'my wife and I both do,' the correct move is: 'Great — is she around? We'd want her on the appointment too since this affects both of you.' The bill photo moment (02:27) confirms she is present in the home. The appointment was booked without confirming her inclusion.

quote: The timing to make it immediately may not be right for us right now, but, like, as long as we can get a plan, like, that we could use for the future, that'd be great.
stewart_read: Paul closes the call with an explicit low-urgency frame: he wants a future plan, not an immediate decision. This is the third touch of the same pattern (04:39, 04:55, 06:53) and Bucky's response is to acknowledge and close. No flip is attempted — 'what you can't afford is your current bill' or a variant anchored to Paul's $120 Dominion bill was available and unused. The appointment is booked but Paul's stated posture makes it a fragile sit.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky may have judged that pushing harder at close would risk the booking entirely. On a cooperative customer who has already agreed to the appointment, this is a defensible read. However, the absence of any urgency creation across the entire call — not just at close — means the strategic-deferral hypothesis does not hold. There was no earlier urgency setup to defer to.
coaching_implication: This is the flip-opportunity-detection coaching moment for this call. Have Bucky identify what Paul's bill pain actually is ($120/month, first month in a new home, Dominion Energy) and construct a flip: 'That $120 is month one — Dominion's rates have gone up every year for the last decade. The plan you're building now is the one that locks in your rate before it goes higher.' The manager should note that this is the universal coaching priority flagged in the codex: zero flip deployments observed across the corpus.

quote: and we will have the estimate and design built for you and then, yeah, answer any questions you'd like. It takes, like, twenty, thirty minutes depending on your questions. We leave, like, forty minute room window.
stewart_read: Bucky's button-up hits the appointment datetime, the phone-call format, the not-driving / email-access anchor, and a time estimate. The 'is that fair?' verbal handshake is absent, and the one-hour-block framing from the codex is replaced with a vaguer 'twenty, thirty minutes... forty minute room window.' The handshake omission is the more significant gap — it is the predictive show-rate element the codex flags explicitly.
coaching_implication: The button-up is mostly complete but missing the 'is that fair?' close. Show Bucky the button_up section and have him add the handshake to his standard close. It is a one-line addition that the codex identifies as predictive of show rate. On a fragile sit like this one, every show-rate lever matters.
